profiles.title: Pedestrian behaviors at and perceptions towards various pedestrian facilities: an examination based on observation and survey data
profiles.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian behaviors and perceptions toward various pedestrian facilities including signalized and unsignalized intersection crosswalks, unsignalized midblock crosswalks, physical barriers (vegetation and concrete walls), midblock crosswalk shelters, colored paving at medians and curbs, and pedestrian warning signs at midblock locations.
profiles.method_summary: Mixed methods approach combining: (1) observational study using video cameras to record pedestrian movements at 8 locations along a 1km divided boulevard section of Grand River Avenue in East Lansing, Michigan; (2) user survey of 711 daily and occasional pedestrians collecting information on crossing patterns, preferences, perceptions of safety, and factors influencing crossing decisions. Spatial and temporal crossing compliance rates calculated from video data and compared with survey responses.
datasets: Video data from 8 video cameras recording pedestrian movements (16-18 hours per session)
User survey responses (n=711) from Michigan State University affiliates
tasks: Assessment of pedestrian perceptions toward pedestrian facilities
Comparison of self-reported behavior with observed behavior
metrics: Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) - percentage of pedestrians crossing within crosswalk area
Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) - percentage complying with WALK signal indication
Pedestrian Crossing Compliance Rate (PCCR) - combined spatial and temporal compliance
baselines: Signalized intersection crosswalks (SCCR: 82.8%)
Unsignalized intersection crosswalks (SCCR: 67.5%)
Non-striped midblock crosswalks (SCCR: 64.2%)

main_claims.claim_text: Unsignalized midblock crosswalks are the most preferred pedestrian facility (83% reported preference) and demonstrate high crossing compliance (71.2%)
Crosswalk location relative to pedestrian origin and destination is the most influential decision factor (90% of respondents)
Pedestrian signal presence significantly influences crossing decisions (74% of survey respondents)
Vegetation and concrete barriers influence crossing decisions of 65% of pedestrians surveyed
Good agreement exists between self-reported survey behavior and observed pedestrian behavior (59% spatial compliance from survey vs 71.4% from movement data)

limitations.text: Sample population primarily consists of university affiliates (MSU students, staff, faculty), which may not represent general pedestrian population
Low survey response rate (17.9%) may introduce non-response bias
Study conducted at a single site (Grand River Avenue), limiting generalizability to other contexts
Data collection limited to weekdays and excluded rainy days and football weekends, potentially missing relevant behavioral variations

future_work: Conduct additional crash and conflict analysis to assess safety value of pedestrian warning signs
Examine relationship between safety and pedestrian acceptance in future research
Perform additional surveys to examine differences between drivers and pedestrians regarding right-of-way at intersections
Develop guidelines for pedestrian control at corridors with major pedestrian crossing volumes
profiles.notes: Study site was a recently renovated 1km section of Grand River Avenue next to Michigan State University campus. The research was sponsored by Michigan Department of Transportation. Key finding: midblock crosswalks more effective in achieving design purpose compared to signalized crosswalks, though signalized crosswalks better for pedestrian channelization. Leading pedestrian intervals recommended for improving pedestrian safety at signalized intersections with significant turning volumes.

profiles.title: Modeling pedestrians' road crossing behavior in traffic system micro-simulation in China
profiles.venue: Transportation Research Part A: Policy and Practice
profiles.research_problem: Pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities differs from developed countries due to high rates of signal non-compliance. Existing foreign micro-simulation tools do not work effectively in China because they cannot represent the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics typical of Chinese cities.
profiles.method_summary: A pedestrian model for traffic system micro-simulation in China that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying and opportunistic. Questionnaires (495 respondents) determined proportions of pedestrian types under different circumstances. Time gap distributions extracted from videotape (989 samples) determined the safety criterion (ΔT) for pedestrians to decide whether to walk or wait when conflicting with vehicle flows. Parameters were calibrated and adjusted to align simulation results with field observations.
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (November 22-23, 2003)
Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples)
Field data from crossings without police on duty (June 2003 to April 2004)
baselines: CORSIM and SIMTRAFFIC (tested but inadequate for Chinese conditions)
Liu et al. (2000) model with fixed pedestrian type proportions
Gap acceptance models (Das et al., 2005; Wan and Rouphail, 2004)

main_claims.claim_text: The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities
Classifying pedestrians into law-obeying and opportunistic types with dynamic proportions based on external factors (policeman, other pedestrians' behavior, vehicle flow) improves simulation accuracy
The model may be applicable to micro-simulation of traffic systems in other developing cities with similar traffic characteristics
After parameter adjustment, simulation results show red light runner proportion ranging from 45% to 83% across different intersections, matching field observations more closely

limitations.text: The model only models pedestrians' decision making of red light running according to the instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles; it does not account for situations when oncoming vehicles change their states (accelerate, decelerate, etc.)
The calculation method of ΔT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical for simulating Chinese city traffic
Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians, and some answers may not reflect actual behavior, leading to parameter calibration uncertainties

future_work: Improve the model to account for pedestrian decision-making when oncoming vehicles change states (acceleration, deceleration)
Develop better calculation methods for ΔT for turning vehicles
Extend the model to handle more complex traffic scenarios in Chinese cities
profiles.notes: The study was conducted in Xi'an, China; Pedestrian red light running rates in Guangzhou reached 66.7% in certain sections according to 2003 report; The Road Traffic Safety Law implemented on May 1, 2004 increased punishment for pedestrian violations; Police presence did not have distinct impact on pedestrian compliance according to questionnaire results

profiles.title: Application of social force model to pedestrian behavior analysis at signalized crosswalk
profiles.venue: Transportation Research Part C: Emerging Technologies
profiles.research_problem: Limited pedestrian behavior models exist for signalized crosswalks, where pedestrian behavior involves group or individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, and response to signal control and crosswalk boundary. The study addresses this gap by developing a microscopic simulation model for pedestrian behavior analysis at signalized intersections.
profiles.method_summary: A modified social force model is developed for pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalks, incorporating five sources of social force: driving force toward destination, repulsive/attractive force from crosswalk boundary, repulsive force from surrounding pedestrians, repulsive force from conflicting vehicles, and attractive force from signal phase. Parameters are separated into measurable (directly estimated from observed trajectory data) and non-measurable (derived by maximum likelihood estimation) groups.
datasets: North crosswalk at Kanayama intersection in Nagoya City, Japan
1352 pedestrian trajectories
124 left-turning vehicle trajectories
97,200 position samples
tasks: Stop/go decision modeling at pedestrian flashing green phase
metrics: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for position (5.7%)
Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for speed (10.5%)
Two-sample t-test for passing position distribution
baselines: Non-route-choice based desired direction model
Classical social force model

main_claims.claim_text: The modified social force model achieves 5.7% MAPE for position estimation and 10.5% MAPE for speed estimation in trajectory reproduction

The desired direction model using Weibull distribution for entering and exit positions, combined with the driving force model, can reasonably reflect pedestrian behavior characteristics at crosswalks as validated by passing position distributions at three cross-sections showing no significant difference between estimated and observed trajectories
The model can reproduce lane-formation phenomenon, with route-choice based model producing 3.5 lanes compared to 2.8 observed, closer than non-route-choice based model (4.1 lanes)

The collision avoidance behavior with conflicting vehicles is well represented, with pedestrians shifting passing positions when encountering left-turning vehicles (observed: 0 to 2m, estimated: 0 to 2.5m)
Separating parameters into measurable and non-measurable groups for calibration is an effective approach, with all parameters showing statistical significance (p < 0.05)

limitations.text: The reaction of pedestrians to vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world (peak shift 2.5m vs 2m observed)
Pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age, gender, and partner relationship are not considered as influencing factors
Model calibrated for left-hand traffic (Japan); may not directly apply to right-hand traffic regions

future_work: Improve pedestrian reaction sensitivity to vehicles to match real-world behavior
Incorporate pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics (age, gender, partner relationship) as influencing factors
Apply more elaborate modeling approach and stricter calibration procedure
Extend model to right-hand traffic contexts
profiles.notes: Study focuses on Japanese signalized intersections with left-hand traffic; Pedestrian flashing green (PFG) phase modeling is crucial for safety assessment; Model considers both repulsive and attractive effects from crosswalk boundary; Stochastic adjustment mechanism developed to avoid pedestrians stopping forever due to social force balance

profiles.title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
profiles.research_problem: Identify potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand intended vehicle behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior. The study addresses the communication gap between driverless vehicles and pedestrians who traditionally rely on eye contact, hand gestures, and other informal communication with human drivers.
profiles.method_summary: Virtual reality experiment using Unity 3D with HTC Vive headset. Thirty participants walked across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. Tested 4 visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' in text, flashing smile, no signal) and 4 audible features (horn, music, verbal message 'safe to cross', no signal) in a 4x4 design. Data collection included: simulator-collected objective measures (crossing time, waiting time, head rotation), baseline and after-study receptivity surveys, demographic questionnaire, Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ), personal innovativeness scale, and feature ratings.
datasets: Custom virtual environment with pedestrian crosswalk (4-way intersection of two-lane streets with two-way stop signs)
tasks: Pedestrian crossing decision-making at unsignalized crosswalk
Evaluation of external interface features for FAV-pedestrian communication
metrics: Feature ratings (1-7 scale for safety feeling)
Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ) - 20 items measuring violation, error, lapse, aggressive, and positive behaviors
baselines: No external feature condition (control)
Previous survey-based studies on pedestrian preferences for external features
Literature on pedestrian-driver communication

main_claims.claim_text: External features on FAVs significantly increase pedestrian receptivity toward autonomous vehicles
Walking silhouette and 'braking' text are the most favored visual interfaces, while verbal message is the preferred audible feature
Females and people aged 30+ react most positively to external features on FAVs
Pedestrians who frequently commit errors or display aggressive road behaviors rate FAV implementation poorly even with external features

limitations.text: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction and always stopping; no other traffic in environment
Features indicated safe crossing conditions only; did not test feature termination/vehicle restart signals
First exposure to FAV may limit generalizability to experienced users
Small sample size (n=30) from U.S. population; limited demographic diversity
VR simulation may not fully replicate real-world pedestrian behavior under threat

future_work: Investigate complex traffic systems with signals, vehicle operators, pedestrian signals, and near-miss conditions
Test start and termination of signals at intersections
Study feasibility and preference from other road-users' perspectives (cyclists, drivers)
Include environmental sounds and distractions
Investigate scenarios where FAV does not stop
Examine long-term effects of FAV exposure on pedestrian behavior
profiles.notes: Study validated VR platform for pedestrian safety research (Deb et al., 2017); FAV defined as SAE Level 5 automation; Walking silhouette rated 5.93/7, 'braking' text rated 5.54/7, verbal message rated 5.91/7; No-signal control condition rated lowest at 1.97/7; Participants with higher personal innovativeness showed lower waiting times (b=-0.17); Pedestrians who violate rules intentionally or get distracted were more cautious with FAVs

profiles.title: Modeling pedestrians' road crossing behavior in traffic system micro-simulation in China
profiles.research_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities, where high rates of signal non-compliance (red light running) are common and differ significantly from pedestrians in developed countries. Existing micro-simulation tools developed abroad fail to accurately describe Chinese traffic situations due to unique characteristics like mixed traffic flows and widespread pedestrian violations.
profiles.method_summary: The paper proposes a pedestrian micro-simulation model that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). The model uses questionnaires to determine the proportions of each pedestrian type under different circumstances (r1, r2), and extracts time gap distributions from videotape to calibrate the gap acceptance criterion (DT) for pedestrians deciding whether to walk or wait when conflicting with vehicle flows. The model framework incorporates external factors including policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, and vehicle flow states.
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (200 students from Xi'an Jiaotong University and 300 random pedestrians); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples extracted for DT calculations)
tasks: Pedestrian red light running behavior modeling
Gap acceptance decision modeling for pedestrian crossing
Simulation of pedestrian-vehicle interactions at signalized intersections
metrics: Proportion of violation followers (pedestrians who follow others to violate)
Comparison of pedestrian arriving interval distributions
baselines: CORSIM and SIMTRAFFIC (tested in Beijing by He, 2003) - found ineffective for pedestrian modeling in China
Previous classification approach by Liu et al. (2000) where proportion of each pedestrian type was simply predetermined before simulation

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities, with simulation results agreeing with field observations after parameter adjustment

Pedestrians can be classified into two types (law-obeying and opportunistic) with proportions that vary based on external factors like policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, and vehicle flow

Questionnaire results show 55% of pedestrians are law-obeying in general conditions, dropping to 17% when other pedestrians violate traffic signal, with 69% of Type I pedestrians potentially following others to violate
The DT (time gap) criterion calculated as DT = T1 - T2 provides an effective measure for pedestrian gap acceptance decisions, with 989 samples collected from videotape establishing the distribution
Initial simulation results using calibrated parameters (r1=0.55, r2=0.69) deviated from field data, requiring adjustment to r2=0.35 to achieve better agreement

limitations.text: The current model only models pedestrian decision making according to instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, without considering scenarios where vehicles change states (accelerate, decelerate, etc.)
The calculation method of DT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical
Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians, and self-reported answers may not reflect actual daily behavior, leading to calibration deviations
The model uses pedestrian's estimation of vehicle instantaneous speed rather than their actual prediction of vehicle future actions

future_work: Extend the model to handle pedestrian behavior when oncoming vehicles change states (acceleration, deceleration)
Improve the DT calculation method for turning vehicles
Further calibration and validation of model parameters for broader application in Chinese cities
profiles.notes: The paper addresses a unique problem specific to developing countries, particularly China, where pedestrian violations are prevalent (23.3% average illegal action rate, up to 66.7% in certain sections). The model may be applicable to micro-simulation of traffic systems in other developing cities with similar characteristics.

profiles.title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
profiles.research_problem: The aim of this research was to identify potential external features on a fully autonomous vehicle (FAV) and investigate which features would help pedestrians understand the intended behavior of a FAV at a crosswalk, improve their receptivity toward FAVs, and affect their crossing behavior. The core problem is that with FAVs, technology is primarily responsible for vehicle control, making interpersonal communication (eye contact, hand gestures) between drivers and pedestrians impossible.
profiles.domain: Autonomous vehicles, human-computer interaction, pedestrian safety, transportation research
profiles.method_summary: A virtual reality experiment using a pedestrian simulator (Unity 3D + HTC Vive headset). Thirty participants walked across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. A 4x4 randomized complete block design tested four visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' in text, flashing smile, no signal) and four audible features (horn sound, music, verbal message 'safe to cross', no signal). Data collection included simulator-collected objective measures (crossing time, waiting time, head tracking) and survey-based subjective measures (receptivity surveys, pedestrian behavior questionnaire, personal innovativeness scale).
method_family: Virtual reality experiment, randomized complete block design
datasets: Custom VR pedestrian simulator (Unity 5 + HTC Vive)
Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ) - 20 items
Pedestrian Receptivity Questionnaire for FAVs (PRQF)
Personal Innovativeness Scale (4 items, adapted from Agarwal & Prasad, 1998)
Simulation Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ)
metrics: Pedestrian behavior subscales (violation, error, lapse, aggression, positive behaviors)
baselines: No external feature (control condition)
Eye contact/hand gestures (informal driver-pedestrian communication)
Traditional traffic signs and signals
Existing research on LED strips, smile displays, walk/don't walk signs

main_claims.claim_text: Pedestrians' receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
Walking silhouette and 'braking' in text were the most favored visual interfaces, while verbal message was the preferred audible feature
Females and people from 30+ age group reacted most positively to the external features
Pedestrians who often commit errors or show aggressive behaviors toward other road users rated FAV implementation poorly, even with external features
Pedestrians who intentionally violate traffic rules and those who get distracted became more cautious in presence of FAVs and appreciated the external features

limitations.text: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction and always stopping; no other traffic included in the virtual environment
Proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only; did not consider negative interfaces or vehicle restart signals
First exposure scenario; complex traffic situations with signals, vehicle operators, positive/negative interfaces, and environmental sounds not investigated
Small sample size (n=30) limits generalizability; participants were mainly from communities surrounding Mississippi State University
Study designed for non-signalized intersection only; results may not generalize to signalized crosswalks

future_work: Investigate complex traffic systems with presence of vehicle operators and pedestrian signals at crosswalks
Include near-miss conditions to observe pedestrian perception of highly rated features
Study start and termination of signals at intersections
Investigate feasibility and preference for reported features from other road-users' perspectives
Test positive and negative interfaces (e.g., 'do not walk' signals)
Examine effects with ambient traffic and environmental sounds
profiles.notes: VR validation study confirmed participants responded similarly in VR traffic environment as in real traffic (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017); The walking silhouette is familiar to pedestrians from existing pedestrian signals, making it easy to interpret; The smile feature was confusing - participants could not determine if it meant 'thank you for not crossing' or 'please cross'; Verbal message considered beneficial for visually impaired pedestrians and in poor visibility conditions; Participants with higher personal innovativeness showed lower waiting time before crossing

profiles.title: An extension of the theory of planned behavior to predict pedestrians' violating crossing behavior using structural equation modeling
profiles.research_problem: Examined pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), extending the theory by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions.
profiles.method_summary: Survey-based study using structural equation modeling (SEM) to analyze data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China. A questionnaire presented a scenario where pedestrians crossed the road violating pedestrian lights at an intersection. The study tested the basic TPB components (instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control) and extended factors (descriptive norm, perceived risk, conformity tendency). Four models were built and compared using goodness of fit indices.
method_family: Structural equation modeling (SEM) with questionnaire survey
datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China
tasks: Predicting pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions
baselines: Model 1: Basic TPB (instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control)
Model 2: Basic TPB + descriptive norm
Model 3: Basic TPB + perceived risk
Model 4: Basic TPB + conformity tendency

main_claims.claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of pedestrians' violating crossing behavior intention across all four models tested
Descriptive norm was a stronger predictor than subjective norm, and when descriptive norm was added to the model, subjective norm became non-significant
Conformity tendency was a significant predictor of violating crossing behavior, indicating that pedestrians are influenced by other pedestrians' violating behavior
Perceived behavioral control was not a significant predictor in any of the models, likely because none of the respondents were physically impaired and the behavior was not challenging enough
Perceived risk was not a significant predictor, suggesting pedestrians failed to adequately perceive the susceptibility and seriousness of the risk pertaining to such violations

limitations.text: Bias of reported behavior, especially negative behavior - actual behavior should be observed for comparison or validation
Relatively small number of items in each construct to maintain internal reliability, which could affect the results
Sample limited to one city (Dalian, China), which may limit generalizability to other cultural contexts

future_work: Increase the number of items in each construct for future studies
Investigate the effects of perceived behavioral control under scenarios representing different levels of difficulty
Extend the study to other cities and cultural contexts for generalizability
profiles.notes: Study found that Model 4 (with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best predictive utility; The survey used a scenario-based questionnaire about crossing against pedestrian lights; All four models showed adequate goodness of fit, with Model 4 achieving the best fit (CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030); Results support using descriptive norms, conformity tendency, and instrumental attitude to predict pedestrians' violations rather than basic TPB alone

profiles.title: Effects of mobile phone distraction on pedestrians' crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection: An outdoor experimental study
profiles.venue: Accident Analysis and Prevention, Volume 115, Pages 170-177
profiles.research_problem: This study investigates how mobile phone distractions (music distraction, phone conversation distraction, and text distraction) affect pedestrians' crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection, comparing the severity of different types of mobile phone distractions on pedestrian safety.
profiles.method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment conducted with 28 college student pedestrians at a signalized intersection. Two HD video cameras recorded crossing behavior, and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker recorded visual attention. Participants completed crossing trials under four conditions: undistracted (S0), music distraction (S1), phone conversation distraction (S2), and text distraction (S3). Eight dependent variables were measured: three crossing behavior indicators (initiate time, look left/right, crossing speed) and five eye movement indicators (pupil diameter, scanning frequency, fixation points, fixation time, fixation duration) across five areas of interest.
tasks: Pedestrian street crossing under different mobile phone distraction conditions
metrics: Initiate time (time between green light and crossing initiation)
Look left and right (frequency while crossing)
Pupil diameter (mean diameter reflecting information processing effort)
Fixation duration (mean duration on each AOI)
baselines: Undistracted condition (S0) - baseline control with no mobile phone use

main_claims.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction, with text distraction significantly increasing initiate time (2.65s vs 0.69s undistracted), decreasing look left/right frequency (2.50 vs 4.46), and reducing crossing speed (1.36 m/s vs 1.46 m/s).

All three mobile phone distraction types significantly reduce visual attention allocation to traffic environment areas, with text distraction showing the most severe impairment: fixation points on phone area increased from 0% (undistracted) to 75.20% while crosswalk area fixation decreased from 65.04% to 16.79%.

Music distraction increases cognitive load (indicated by larger pupil diameter: 2.86mm vs 2.75mm undistracted) and reduces scanning frequency (6.00 vs 6.82) and fixation points toward traffic signal area, but has minimal effect on crossing speed compared to undistracted condition.

Phone conversation distraction causes pedestrians to cross more slowly (1.37 m/s vs 1.46 m/s), direct fewer fixation points to right traffic area, and spend less fixation time and average fixation duration on left traffic area compared to undistracted condition.

Text distraction involves both cognitive and visual distraction, occupying most of limited visual attention and causing pedestrians to switch, distribute and maintain less visual attention on the traffic environment compared to other distraction conditions.

limitations.text: Small sample size (n=28) limited to college students, restricting generalizability to broader pedestrian population
Participants restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation; may not reflect real-world mobile phone use patterns
Data collected at only one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume (<5 vehicles/minute), limiting ecological validity for complex traffic environments
Experimental design differs from daily life conditions: unfamiliar phone (Huawei P9), mental arithmetic tasks for conversation/texting may not accurately represent typical mobile phone use
Eye-tracking technology limitations: participants did not wear glasses, which may exclude populations with vision impairments who also use mobile phones

future_work: Extend research to more diverse samples beyond college students to improve generalizability
Test approach in other environments such as non-signalized intersections or high-traffic volume intersections
Investigate other mobile phone tasks such as mobile internet browsing for fuller understanding of distraction effects
Explore design elements and driver regulations that could protect distracted pedestrians
Develop and evaluate intervention strategies to reduce distracted walking
profiles.notes: Study used 40-km/h speed limited intersection with 14-meter-wide crosswalk, 21-second green light and 39-second red light; Eye tracker: Tobii Pro Glasses 2 at 50-HZ sampling rate; Five Areas of Interest (AOIs) defined: mobile phone area, crosswalk area, traffic signal area, right traffic area, left traffic area; All three distraction conditions showed statistically significant differences (p<0.05) from undistracted baseline on most measures; Supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China and Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation

profiles.title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
profiles.research_problem: Identifying factors that affect the propensity of red light running violation of pedestrians at signalized intersections, examining both personal factors (demographics, behavior) and environmental factors (presence of other pedestrians, signal time, traffic condition)
profiles.method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong, capturing crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during 652 signal cycles. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian behavior.
method_family: Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with normally distributed random parameters)
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong during 652 signal cycles
tasks: Pedestrian red light running violation analysis
metrics: Binary outcome (red light running vs. not running)
baselines: Model 1: Random parameter logit without interaction effects
Model 2: Random parameter logit with interaction effects (selected as better performing model)

main_claims.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667)
Older pedestrians have higher propensity of red light running compared to adults (odds ratio = 1.208), contrary to some previous studies
Presence of other pedestrians violating red light significantly increases individual red light running propensity (odds ratio = 4.023), demonstrating social norm influence
Increase in time to green and red time both increase red light running propensity, while higher traffic volume and percentage of heavy vehicles reduce it
Significant interaction effects exist between gender and traffic volume, gender and others' violation behavior, elderly and companion presence, on red light running propensity

limitations.text: Study focuses on factors affecting red light running propensity but does not assess safety outcomes (crash risk)
Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior was not measured despite its relevance to crash risk and severity
Random parameter distribution assumption (normal) may not capture all unobserved heterogeneity; other distributions were not tested
Study limited to urban crosswalks in Hong Kong; generalizability to other contexts with different traffic signal systems (e.g., countdown displays) may be limited

future_work: Explore interactions between vehicular speed, pedestrian characteristics, and red light running decision using comprehensive traffic flow data
Investigate effectiveness of variable pedestrian signal time responsive to pedestrian volume
Evaluate pedestrian signal countdown device effectiveness in reducing red light running
Develop attitudinal models to gauge effects of social norms, safety perception, and anticipated traffic conditions
Extend analysis to include crash risk outcomes
profiles.notes: Study addresses gap in literature by combining individual-level (personal demographics, behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors; Novel contribution includes examining influence of social norms through presence/behavior of other pedestrians in same cycle; Hong Kong pedestrian signal system has three phases: steady green, flashing green, and steady red (no countdown display); Model 2 with interaction effects showed superior fit (lower AIC: 2750.1 vs 2776.6)

profiles.title: A model of pedestrians' intended waiting times for street crossings at signalized intersections
profiles.research_problem: The paper addresses the inadequate modeling of pedestrian waiting times at signalized intersections. Existing models (exponential distribution) fail to capture the bounded nature of waiting times during the red-man phase and cannot represent the observed U-shaped distribution of pedestrian behavior.
profiles.method_summary: The paper proposes a statistical model for pedestrian waiting times based on a bounded Pareto distribution extended to account for pedestrian-vehicle interaction via statistical unconditioning. Pedestrians are classified into risk-taking and risk-averse categories based on their effective critical headway (ECH) and sensitivity to excess headway. The model results in a mixture distribution with four components that can produce U-shaped, J-shaped, or L-shaped distributions depending on traffic conditions and pedestrian types.
datasets: Field data from signalized intersection in Kunming, China (283 valid observations)
tasks: Modeling pedestrian intended waiting times during red-man phase
baselines: Exponential distribution (standard waiting time model)
Bounded Pareto distribution
Weibull and gamma distributions (used in survival analysis)

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed model produces a U-shaped distribution that characterizes pedestrian street-crossing behavior, showing large proportions of pedestrians who cross immediately and those willing to wait for the entire red-man phase
The U-shaped distribution can reduce to J-shaped or L-shaped distributions under certain traffic scenarios depending on pedestrian types and traffic conditions
The model adequately fits empirical field data from Kunming, China (chi-square = 3.51, not rejected at 5% significance level)
Risk-taking pedestrians have smaller intended waiting times than risk-averse pedestrians (median relationships satisfy w(1)0.5 < w(3)0.5 + w(4)0.5)

limitations.text: The model focuses on univariate analysis with only pedestrian waiting time as the explicit variable
Data collected from only one intersection in Kunming, China - generalizability to other locations may be limited
Pedestrian classification into only two categories (risk-taking and risk-averse) may oversimplify real-world behavioral diversity

future_work: Develop a multivariate modeling approach to simultaneously analyze multiple risk factors for pedestrian waiting times at signalized intersections, extending beyond the univariate model presented in this paper.
profiles.notes: The model introduces the effective critical headway (ECH) concept - the minimum vehicle time headway required by a pedestrian to cross safely. The sensitivity coefficient b relates the excess headway to waiting time behavior. The EM algorithm is used for parameter estimation with BIC for model selection. Empirical findings show young pedestrians tend to be risk-takers while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse, with 13.8% crossing immediately and 50.6% willing to wait for entire red phase.

profiles.title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
profiles.research_problem: The study investigates factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, which is a critical contributor to pedestrian-involved traffic crashes. The research addresses the gap in understanding how individual characteristics, trip features, and pedestrian attitudes collectively influence red-light running decisions.
profiles.method_summary: A questionnaire-based SP (Stated Preference) survey was conducted in Hefei, China, collecting data on pedestrian socioeconomic characteristics, trip features, and attitudes toward crossing facilities. Using 631 valid samples from six signalized intersections, a binomial logistic regression model was developed. The Score test was used for variable selection at the 95% confidence level, followed by iterative calculation to identify significant predictors.
datasets: Survey data collected from Hefei, China (May-June 2015); 631 valid samples from 6 signalized intersections
tasks: Analyze factors influencing pedestrian red-light running behavior at signalized intersections
metrics: Binary outcome (run red-light vs. not run); Odds ratios; Statistical significance (p<0.05)
baselines: Reference categories: trip purpose = going to work/school; time period = dawn; age group = under 12 years

main_claims.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
Strolling pedestrians are significantly more likely to have safe crossing behavior compared to those going to work or school (odds ratio = 0.394, p=0.019)
Pedestrians are most likely to run red-lights during daytime, followed by dusk and night, with dawn having the lowest probability
Pedestrians who are in a hurry are significantly more likely to run red-lights (odds ratio = 0.299, p<0.001), reflecting time pressure as a key behavioral factor
Pedestrians who are affected by road facility quality are more likely to run red-lights at intersections without monitoring equipment (odds ratio = 0.483, p=0.001)

limitations.text: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences
Study only examined signalized intersections, excluding other crossing facility types such as midblock crossings with/without buttons
Analysis focused on individual pedestrian behaviors, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and group dynamics
Sample size (631) may be insufficient for fully capturing regional variations within the city

future_work: Collect data from multiple intersections in other Chinese cities to validate findings
Extend analysis to other crossing facility types (midblock with/without crosswalk buttons)
Investigate impacts of pedestrian platoons and group behavior on crossing decisions
Compare results across different countries to identify cultural and driving factors affecting red-light running behaviors
Implement and evaluate countermeasures such as variable speed limits and improved lighting
profiles.notes: The study addresses a critical traffic safety issue in China where pedestrian-involved crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. The binomial logistic model provides a probability prediction function (Equation 3) that can be used to identify high-risk pedestrian groups and intersections. Key policy implications include improving road crossing facilities, implementing traffic signal控制系统, and strengthening law enforcement in rush hours and areas with faster life rhythms.

profiles.title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
profiles.research_problem: Identify factors affecting pedestrian red light running violations at signalized intersections, examining both personal factors (demographics, behavior) and environmental factors (presence and behavior of other pedestrians, signal time, traffic condition)
profiles.method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal across 652 cycles were captured. Random parameter logit model developed to measure association between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian decision-making.
method_family: Random parameter logit model (binary choice model with random parameters)
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong
652 signal cycles captured

Six locations: Prince Edward Road West j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Sai Yee Street, Tonkin Street j/w Shun Ning Road, Hung Hom South Road j/w Po loi Street, To Kwa Wan Road j/w Chi Kiang Street
tasks: Examine factors affecting pedestrian red light running decision
Model individual pedestrian decision of red light running violation at signalized intersections
metrics: Binary outcome (run red light vs not run)
baselines: Model 1: Random parameter logit without interaction effects
Model 2: Random parameter logit with interaction effects (selected as better fitting model with lower AIC: 2750.1 vs 2776.6)

main_claims.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (b=-0.433, odds ratio=0.649), and this effect varies among individuals as the random component is statistically significant
Presence of a companion significantly reduces red light running propensity (b=-0.984, odds ratio=0.374), and elderly pedestrians with companions show even lower propensity due to companion compliance

Social influences strongly affect red light running: presence of other violators increases propensity (b=1.029, odds ratio=2.798) while more pedestrians waiting reduces it (b=-0.394, odds ratio=0.674)
Time to green and red time are positively associated with red light running propensity, indicating pedestrians become impatient with longer waiting times
Higher traffic volume and percentage of heavy vehicles reduce red light running propensity (b=-0.021 and b=-0.018 respectively), showing pedestrians are more cautious when facing more traffic

limitations.text: Study focuses on pedestrian factors only; safety outcome (associated crash risk) is out of study scope
Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior was not assessed despite being correlated with crash risk and severity
Data collected only in Hong Kong urban areas; findings may not generalize to other regions with different traffic cultures, signal systems, or pedestrian demographics
No pedestrian signal countdown device in Hong Kong limits generalizability to cities with such devices

future_work: Explore effectiveness of pedestrian signal countdown devices in improving pedestrian perception and reducing red light running
Investigate variable pedestrian signal time responsive to pedestrian volume
Develop attitudinal models to gauge effects of social norms, safety perception, and anticipated traffic conditions on red light running propensity
Examine interactions between vehicular speed, pedestrian characteristics, and red light running decisions with comprehensive traffic flow data
Extend surveys to crosswalks with varying speed limits
Investigate crash risk outcomes associated with red light running behavior

profiles.title: Modeling the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian crossing behavior at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
profiles.research_problem: The paper addresses the interaction between vehicles and pedestrians at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, where drivers and pedestrians must make simultaneous decisions about yielding and gap acceptance. While pedestrian gap acceptance, vehicle yielding behavior, and microscopic traffic flow models have been studied separately, no existing work converges these behaviors into a car-following model to analyze traffic flow effects.
profiles.method_summary: The study extends the Full Velocity Difference (FVD) car-following model by integrating probabilistic logit models for vehicle yielding (VY) and pedestrian gap acceptance (PGA) behaviors. The VY logit model considers gap size, number of lanes, number of waiting pedestrians, median location, and e-police presence. The PGA logit model considers gap size, crossing distance, waiting time, and pedestrian position. The combined model simulates vehicle-pedestrian interactions through time-step simulation in MATLAB, determining whether vehicles yield based on pedestrian crossing status and whether pedestrians accept gaps based on the logit probability.
method_family: Microscopic Traffic Flow Model / Car-Following Model / Full Velocity Difference (FVD) Model Extension
datasets: Data from 13 midblock crosswalk locations in Shanghai, China (for VY and PGA model development)
Empirical data from 295 Zhenhua Rd. and 1565 Lingshi Rd. in Shanghai (for model validation)
tasks: Traffic flow analysis at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
Sensitivity analysis of traffic and geometric factors
metrics: Relative error between simulated and surveyed values
baselines: Traditional FVD car-following model without vehicle-pedestrian interaction
Separate VY and PGA models from previous studies

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed model successfully describes the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
Pedestrian waiting time increases with the number of lanes and vehicular volume but decreases with pedestrian volume
Vehicular delay increases more rapidly than pedestrian waiting time with the increase of vehicle volume, pedestrian volume, and number of lanes
The model validation shows maximum relative error less than 10% between simulated and surveyed pedestrian waiting times, with no significant difference (p > 0.05) in Wilcoxon signed-rank test
Signal control is recommended when vehicle volume exceeds 480 veh/h/ln or pedestrian volume exceeds 500 ped/h, or when more than 4 traffic lanes are present

limitations.text: The model does not consider illegal pedestrian crossing behavior, which is typical in developing countries
The study does not account for the combined effect of midblock crosswalks and adjacent intersections on traffic flow
The model uses parameters calibrated for Shanghai, China, which may not generalize to other regions with different traffic cultures

future_work: Extend the model to study illegal pedestrian crossing at locations without designated crosswalks
Incorporate the combined effect of midblock crosswalks and adjacent intersections
Investigate traffic arrival distribution effects when considering distance to adjacent intersections
profiles.notes: The paper received funding from National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 71971140); Published in Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour; The VY model was developed by Malenje et al. (2019) and PGA model by Zhao et al. (2019); The LOS B threshold for vehicular delay at signalized intersections (20 seconds) from Highway Capacity Manual 2010 was used as the criteria for recommending signal control

profiles.title: Study on pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections
profiles.venue: Journal of Traffic and Transportation Engineering (English Edition)
profiles.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections under mixed traffic conditions, including crossing speed variations, signal compliance rates, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions to identify influencing factors for improving pedestrian safety
profiles.method_summary: Data collected through videography at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai, India. 775 pedestrian samples were observed during morning and evening peak hours. Statistical tests including ANOVA, Pearson's correlation coefficient, and Student t-test were performed. Logistic regression models were developed to model odds of pedestrian noncompliance and pedestrian-vehicular interaction.
method_family: Statistical analysis (ANOVA, Pearson correlation, t-test, logistic regression)
datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai (Swami Vivekanand Junction, Mahim Junction, Holkar Junction)
tasks: Develop logistic regression models for noncompliance and interaction prediction
metrics: Pedestrian compliance rate (percentage crossing during green phase)
baselines: Indian Road Congress (IRC) standard walking speed of 1.2 m/s
Crossing speed values from China (1.33 m/s)

main_claims.claim_text: Male pedestrians cross faster than female pedestrians at signalized intersections
Pedestrian age and departure signal phase significantly affect crossing speed variations (ANOVA F values > table value, P < 0.05)
Gender and group size are significant factors affecting pedestrian compliance with traffic signals
Approaching vehicle type and suitable gap between pedestrian and vehicle significantly influence pedestrian-vehicular interactions
Design crossing speed should be 0.95 m/s for old pedestrians and 1.12 m/s for adult pedestrians, lower than the IRC standard of 1.2 m/s

limitations.text: Sample size of 775 pedestrians across 3 intersections may not fully represent all traffic conditions
Study conducted in Mumbai, India only - results may not generalize to other regions with different traffic patterns
Crossing speed and compliance behavior may vary significantly in non-peak hours

future_work: Expand sample size for more robust statistical results
Include pedestrian arrival pattern analysis
Develop pedestrian delay models
Develop pedestrian level of service models
Extend research to additional locations for broader applicability
profiles.notes: Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF) was newly established as a ratio of crossing speed variation to average crossing speed; Logistic regression models showed male pedestrians have higher noncompliance rates; Adult pedestrians have higher odds (8.074) of vehicle interaction compared to children; Overall pedestrian compliance rate was only 44%, with significant variation across junctions (34%-64%)

profiles.title: A probabilistic model of pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections for connected vehicles
profiles.research_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of predicting pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections to prevent collisions between turning vehicles and pedestrians. The goal is to recognize pedestrian crossing decisions (cross or wait) and motion types (standing, walking, running) within seconds from traffic signal and pedestrian position information, which can be obtained via Connected Vehicle technology.
profiles.method_summary: The authors propose a Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) model that probabilistically integrates relationships among intersection context information (traffic signal phases) and pedestrian behavior (decisions and motion types). The model follows the flow of pedestrian behavior: assessing the situation (signal phase, position relative to crosswalk), making decisions (cross/wait), determining motion type (standing/walking/running), and executing physical movement. Particle filter is used for Bayesian filtering to estimate states including position, crossing decision, and motion type.
method_family: Probabilistic Graphical Models (Dynamic Bayesian Network) with Particle Filter inference
datasets: Real traffic data collected from a vehicle at a signalized intersection in Japan - 289 pedestrian sequences (251 cross-scenarios, 38 wait-scenarios) with manually labeled pedestrian grounding points, motion types, crosswalk edge positions, and traffic signal phases
tasks: Pedestrian crossing decision recognition (cross vs wait)
baselines: Iryo-Asano et al. (2015) - logistic regression model using distance to crosswalk and traveling speed at onset of PFG

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed DBN-based system can correctly recognize pedestrian crossing decisions with >85% accuracy after 2 seconds of observation for cross decisions and 3-4 seconds for wait decisions
The model integrates contextual information (traffic signal phases) with pedestrian behavior modeling in a probabilistic framework, enabling estimation of latent states like crossing intention
The system provides probabilistic outputs (confidence values) rather than binary classifications, allowing vehicle systems to make varied decisions based on confidence levels
Particle filter inference with the proposed DBN can reduce positioning errors from noisy measurements, especially in high-noise conditions (rn=1.0m)

limitations.text: The model assumes pedestrians make crossing decisions only at the onset of PFG, which may not reflect real-world behavior where pedestrians can change decisions later
Running motion is difficult to distinguish from walking due to overlapping speed distributions; recognition rate for running is only 35-56% compared to 90% for walking
The model only predicts straight trajectories, cannot handle curved pedestrian trajectories which occur in the dataset
Computation time is relatively high (5.1s to process 1.0s of data with 2000 particles), requiring optimization for real-time applications
The model does not account for additional contextual factors such as the presence and positions of turning vehicles, which affect pedestrian decisions

future_work: Incorporate additional contextual information such as existence and positions of turning vehicles to improve decision modeling
Extend the model to predict curved pedestrian trajectories by learning turning motion as a spatial feature
Improve computational efficiency through multi-threaded and parallel processing implementation
Add more explanatory variables to the decision model (e.g., crosswalk length, time elapsed from PFG onset for pedestrians entering after PFG start)
profiles.notes: This paper belongs to the Virtual Special Issue on: Connected Autonomous Vehicles; The study focuses on left-hand traffic context (Japan) with near-side pedestrians approaching left-turning vehicles; Pedestrian signals in Japan have three phases: PG (pedestrian green), PFG (pedestrian flashing green), and PR (pedestrian red); The model uses gamma distributions for context-dependent speed modeling and combines constant-speed with context-speed models

profiles.title: Modeling pedestrian crossing speed profiles considering speed change behavior for the safety assessment of signalized intersections
profiles.research_problem: Pedestrian safety at signalized intersections is compromised by unpredictable sudden speed changes (acceleration/deceleration) that pedestrians exhibit while crossing. These behavioral changes cannot be anticipated by conflicting vehicles, leading to hazardous situations. The study aims to quantitatively model these sudden speed changes to improve safety assessment at signalized crosswalks under uncongested conditions.
profiles.method_summary: The study develops a probabilistic discrete choice model to determine acceleration/deceleration timing using stepwise speed functions. The speed change event occurrence is modeled as a function of necessary walking speed to complete crossing before red interval ends, current speed, and presence of turning vehicles. The amount of speed change is modeled using regression analysis (linear regression for acceleration, normal distribution for deceleration). Monte Carlo simulation generates entire pedestrian speed profiles. The model is integrated with vehicle maneuver models for safety assessment using Post Encroachment Time (PET).
method_family: Discrete choice modeling, Regression analysis, Monte Carlo simulation
datasets: Video data from 5 crosswalks at 3 intersections in Nagoya City, Japan (Kanayama East/North, Ueda East/South, Fushimi South)
tasks: Speed change event prediction (acceleration/deceleration timing and magnitude)
Vehicle-pedestrian conflict simulation for safety assessment
metrics: Modified rho-squared (0.431 for speed change event model)
Modified R-squared (0.765 for acceleration sub-model, 0.725 for deceleration sub-model)
baselines: Constant speed model (assuming pedestrians maintain initial speed throughout crossing)

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed model can represent pedestrian travel time distribution more accurately than the constant speed model

The difference between necessary speed to complete crossing (Vnec) and current speed, conflict area dummy variable, and flashing green indication have significant impacts on acceleration and deceleration choices
The speed change model provides significantly higher vehicle yield rates compared to constant speed assumptions
Pedestrian speed change behavior significantly affects PET distributions in safety assessment

limitations.text: The model tends to underestimate the probability of acceleration events (43.8% error in detecting actual acceleration events)
Pedestrian-pedestrian interaction is not considered due to low pedestrian traffic volumes at study sites
Pedestrian characteristics such as age, gender, and distraction from devices are not incorporated
The model cannot capture real interaction between pedestrians and turning vehicles (only conflict area location is considered)
Limited sample size from only five sites prevented some expected variables (PFG/PG length, crosswalk length, elapsed time from PFG onset) from being significant

future_work: Incorporate pedestrian characteristics (age, gender) into the model
Investigate pedestrian distraction effects from smartphones and gadgets
Improve accuracy of individual speed-change event detection
Include pedestrian-pedestrian interactions
Use more precise tracking techniques like laser scanning instead of manual video tracking
Develop real-time information provision systems for vehicles
Apply model to autonomous vehicle pedestrian detection and avoidance maneuvers
profiles.notes: The study was supported by the Memory of Understanding between Qatar Transportation and Traffic Safety Center at Qatar University and the International Center of Urban Safety Engineering at the University of Tokyo. Data was collected using image-processing software TrafficAnalyzer with measurement errors between 0.14m and 0.38m.

matrix.title: Pedestrian behaviors at and perceptions towards various pedestrian facilities: an examination based on observation and survey data
matrix.authors: V.P. Sisiopiku; D. Akin
matrix.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian behaviors and perceptions toward various pedestrian facilities including signalized and unsignalized intersection crosswalks, unsignalized midblock crosswalks, physical barriers (vegetation and concrete walls), midblock crosswalk shelters, colored paving at medians and curbs, and pedestrian warning signs at midblock locations.
matrix.method_summary: Mixed methods approach combining: (1) observational study using video cameras to record pedestrian movements at 8 locations along a 1km divided boulevard section of Grand River Avenue in East Lansing, Michigan; (2) user survey of 711 daily and occasional pedestrians collecting information on crossing patterns, preferences, perceptions of safety, and factors influencing crossing decisions. Spatial and temporal crossing compliance rates calculated from video data and compared with survey responses.
matrix.method_family: Mixed Methods - Field Observation + Survey
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian crossing compliance analysis; Assessment of pedestrian perceptions toward pedestrian facilities; Comparison of self-reported behavior with observed behavior
matrix.datasets: Video data from 8 video cameras recording pedestrian movements (16-18 hours per session); User survey responses (n=711) from Michigan State University affiliates
matrix.metrics: Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) - percentage of pedestrians crossing within crosswalk area; Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) - percentage complying with WALK signal indication; Pedestrian Crossing Compliance Rate (PCCR) - combined spatial and temporal compliance
matrix.claim_text: Unsignalized midblock crosswalks are the most preferred pedestrian facility (83% reported preference) and demonstrate high crossing compliance (71.2%)
matrix.limitations: Sample population primarily consists of university affiliates (MSU students, staff, faculty), which may not represent general pedestrian population | Low survey response rate (17.9%) may introduce non-response bias | Study conducted at a single site (Grand River Avenue), limiting generalizability to other contexts | Data collection limited to weekdays and excluded rainy days and football weekends, potentially missing relevant behavioral variations
matrix.notes: Study site was a recently renovated 1km section of Grand River Avenue next to Michigan State University campus. The research was sponsored by Michigan Department of Transportation. Key finding: midblock crosswalks more effective in achieving design purpose compared to signalized crosswalks, though signalized crosswalks better for pedestrian channelization. Leading pedestrian intervals recommended for improving pedestrian safety at signalized intersections with significant turning volumes.

matrix.title: Modeling pedestrians' road crossing behavior in traffic system micro-simulation in China
matrix.authors: Jianguo Yang; Wen Deng; Jinmei Wang; Qingfeng Li; Zhaoan Wang
matrix.venue: Transportation Research Part A: Policy and Practice
matrix.research_problem: Pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities differs from developed countries due to high rates of signal non-compliance. Existing foreign micro-simulation tools do not work effectively in China because they cannot represent the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics typical of Chinese cities.
matrix.method_summary: A pedestrian model for traffic system micro-simulation in China that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying and opportunistic. Questionnaires (495 respondents) determined proportions of pedestrian types under different circumstances. Time gap distributions extracted from videotape (989 samples) determined the safety criterion (ΔT) for pedestrians to decide whether to walk or wait when conflicting with vehicle flows. Parameters were calibrated and adjusted to align simulation results with field observations.
matrix.method_family: Microscopic behavioral simulation model
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian road crossing behavior modeling; Traffic micro-simulation; Pedestrian violation behavior analysis

matrix.title: Application of social force model to pedestrian behavior analysis at signalized crosswalk
matrix.authors: Weiliang Zeng; Peng Chen; Hideki Nakamura; Miho Iryo-Asano
matrix.venue: Transportation Research Part C: Emerging Technologies
matrix.research_problem: Limited pedestrian behavior models exist for signalized crosswalks, where pedestrian behavior involves group or individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, and response to signal control and crosswalk boundary. The study addresses this gap by developing a microscopic simulation model for pedestrian behavior analysis at signalized intersections.
matrix.method_summary: A modified social force model is developed for pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalks, incorporating five sources of social force: driving force toward destination, repulsive/attractive force from crosswalk boundary, repulsive force from surrounding pedestrians, repulsive force from conflicting vehicles, and attractive force from signal phase. Parameters are separated into measurable (directly estimated from observed trajectory data) and non-measurable (derived by maximum likelihood estimation) groups.
matrix.method_family: Social force model (modified)
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian trajectory estimation; Stop/go decision modeling at pedestrian flashing green phase; Collision avoidance behavior with vehicles; Lane formation phenomenon reproduction; Passing position distribution validation
matrix.datasets: North crosswalk at Kanayama intersection in Nagoya City, Japan; 1352 pedestrian trajectories; 124 left-turning vehicle trajectories; 97,200 position samples
matrix.metrics: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for position (5.7%); Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for speed (10.5%); Two-sample t-test for passing position distribution; Lane formation count comparison
matrix.claim_text: The modified social force model achieves 5.7% MAPE for position estimation and 10.5% MAPE for speed estimation in trajectory reproduction
matrix.limitations: The reaction of pedestrians to vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world (peak shift 2.5m vs 2m observed) | Pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age, gender, and partner relationship are not considered as influencing factors | Model calibrated for left-hand traffic (Japan); may not directly apply to right-hand traffic regions
matrix.notes: Study focuses on Japanese signalized intersections with left-hand traffic; Pedestrian flashing green (PFG) phase modeling is crucial for safety assessment; Model considers both repulsive and attractive effects from crosswalk boundary; Stochastic adjustment mechanism developed to avoid pedestrians stopping forever due to social force balance

matrix.title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
matrix.authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
matrix.research_problem: Identify potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand intended vehicle behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior. The study addresses the communication gap between driverless vehicles and pedestrians who traditionally rely on eye contact, hand gestures, and other informal communication with human drivers.
matrix.method_summary: Virtual reality experiment using Unity 3D with HTC Vive headset. Thirty participants walked across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. Tested 4 visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' in text, flashing smile, no signal) and 4 audible features (horn, music, verbal message 'safe to cross', no signal) in a 4x4 design. Data collection included: simulator-collected objective measures (crossing time, waiting time, head rotation), baseline and after-study receptivity surveys, demographic questionnaire, Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ), personal innovativeness scale, and feature ratings.
matrix.method_family: Laboratory experiment/VR simulation
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian crossing decision-making at unsignalized crosswalk; Evaluation of external interface features for FAV-pedestrian communication
matrix.datasets: Custom virtual environment with pedestrian crosswalk (4-way intersection of two-lane streets with two-way stop signs)
matrix.metrics: Feature ratings (1-7 scale for safety feeling); Crossing time (seconds); Waiting time before entering crosswalk (seconds); Pedestrian Receptivity Questionnaire for FAVs (PRQF); Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ) - 20 items measuring violation, error, lapse, aggressive, and positive behaviors; Personal Innovativeness Scale (4 items); Simulation Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ)
matrix.claim_text: External features on FAVs significantly increase pedestrian receptivity toward autonomous vehicles
matrix.limitations: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction and always stopping; no other traffic in environment | Features indicated safe crossing conditions only; did not test feature termination/vehicle restart signals | First exposure to FAV may limit generalizability to experienced users | Small sample size (n=30) from U.S. population; limited demographic diversity | VR simulation may not fully replicate real-world pedestrian behavior under threat
matrix.notes: Study validated VR platform for pedestrian safety research (Deb et al., 2017); FAV defined as SAE Level 5 automation; Walking silhouette rated 5.93/7, 'braking' text rated 5.54/7, verbal message rated 5.91/7; No-signal control condition rated lowest at 1.97/7; Participants with higher personal innovativeness showed lower waiting times (b=-0.17); Pedestrians who violate rules intentionally or get distracted were more cautious with FAVs

matrix.title: Modeling pedestrians' road crossing behavior in traffic system micro-simulation in China
matrix.authors: Jianguo Yang; Wen Deng; Jinmei Wang; Qingfeng Li; Zhaoan Wang
matrix.research_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities, where high rates of signal non-compliance (red light running) are common and differ significantly from pedestrians in developed countries. Existing micro-simulation tools developed abroad fail to accurately describe Chinese traffic situations due to unique characteristics like mixed traffic flows and widespread pedestrian violations.
matrix.method_summary: The paper proposes a pedestrian micro-simulation model that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). The model uses questionnaires to determine the proportions of each pedestrian type under different circumstances (r1, r2), and extracts time gap distributions from videotape to calibrate the gap acceptance criterion (DT) for pedestrians deciding whether to walk or wait when conflicting with vehicle flows. The model framework incorporates external factors including policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, and vehicle flow states.
matrix.method_family: Microscopic simulation / Agent-based modeling
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian red light running behavior modeling; Gap acceptance decision modeling for pedestrian crossing; Simulation of pedestrian-vehicle interactions at signalized intersections
matrix.datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (200 students from Xi'an Jiaotong University and 300 random pedestrians); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples extracted for DT calculations)
matrix.metrics: Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers (pedestrians who follow others to violate); Comparison of pedestrian arriving interval distributions
matrix.claim_text: The proposed model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities, with simulation results agreeing with field observations after parameter adjustment
matrix.limitations: The current model only models pedestrian decision making according to instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, without considering scenarios where vehicles change states (accelerate, decelerate, etc.) | The calculation method of DT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians, and self-reported answers may not reflect actual daily behavior, leading to calibration deviations | The model uses pedestrian's estimation of vehicle instantaneous speed rather than their actual prediction of vehicle future actions
matrix.notes: The paper addresses a unique problem specific to developing countries, particularly China, where pedestrian violations are prevalent (23.3% average illegal action rate, up to 66.7% in certain sections). The model may be applicable to micro-simulation of traffic systems in other developing cities with similar characteristics.

matrix.title: Modeling pedestrians' road crossing behavior in traffic system micro-simulation in China
matrix.authors: Jianguo Yang; Wen Deng; Jinmei Wang; Qingfeng Li; Zhaoan Wang
matrix.research_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities, where high rates of signal non-compliance (red light running) are common and differ significantly from pedestrians in developed countries. Existing micro-simulation tools developed abroad fail to accurately describe Chinese traffic situations due to unique characteristics like mixed traffic flows and widespread pedestrian violations.
matrix.method_summary: The paper proposes a pedestrian micro-simulation model that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). The model uses questionnaires to determine the proportions of each pedestrian type under different circumstances (r1, r2), and extracts time gap distributions from videotape to calibrate the gap acceptance criterion (DT) for pedestrians deciding whether to walk or wait when conflicting with vehicle flows. The model framework incorporates external factors including policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, and vehicle flow states.
matrix.method_family: Microscopic simulation / Agent-based modeling
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian red light running behavior modeling; Gap acceptance decision modeling for pedestrian crossing; Simulation of pedestrian-vehicle interactions at signalized intersections
matrix.datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (200 students from Xi'an Jiaotong University and 300 random pedestrians); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples extracted for DT calculations)
matrix.metrics: Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers (pedestrians who follow others to violate); Comparison of pedestrian arriving interval distributions
matrix.claim_text: Pedestrians can be classified into two types (law-obeying and opportunistic) with proportions that vary based on external factors like policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, and vehicle flow
matrix.limitations: The current model only models pedestrian decision making according to instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, without considering scenarios where vehicles change states (accelerate, decelerate, etc.) | The calculation method of DT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians, and self-reported answers may not reflect actual daily behavior, leading to calibration deviations | The model uses pedestrian's estimation of vehicle instantaneous speed rather than their actual prediction of vehicle future actions
matrix.notes: The paper addresses a unique problem specific to developing countries, particularly China, where pedestrian violations are prevalent (23.3% average illegal action rate, up to 66.7% in certain sections). The model may be applicable to micro-simulation of traffic systems in other developing cities with similar characteristics.
