matrix.title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
matrix.authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
matrix.research_problem: The aim of this research was to identify potential external features on a fully autonomous vehicle (FAV) and investigate which features would help pedestrians understand the intended behavior of a FAV at a crosswalk, improve their receptivity toward FAVs, and affect their crossing behavior. The core problem is that with FAVs, technology is primarily responsible for vehicle control, making interpersonal communication (eye contact, hand gestures) between drivers and pedestrians impossible.
matrix.method_summary: A virtual reality experiment using a pedestrian simulator (Unity 3D + HTC Vive headset). Thirty participants walked across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. A 4x4 randomized complete block design tested four visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' in text, flashing smile, no signal) and four audible features (horn sound, music, verbal message 'safe to cross', no signal). Data collection included simulator-collected objective measures (crossing time, waiting time, head tracking) and survey-based subjective measures (receptivity surveys, pedestrian behavior questionnaire, personal innovativeness scale).
matrix.method_family: Virtual reality experiment, randomized complete block design
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian-FAV interaction at crosswalk; Feature preference rating; Crossing decision making; Receptivity assessment before/after exposure
matrix.datasets: Custom VR pedestrian simulator (Unity 5 + HTC Vive); Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ) - 20 items; Pedestrian Receptivity Questionnaire for FAVs (PRQF); Personal Innovativeness Scale (4 items, adapted from Agarwal & Prasad, 1998); Simulation Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ)
matrix.metrics: Feature ratings (7-point scale); Crossing time; Waiting time before entering crosswalk; Receptivity scores (scenario-based surveys); Pedestrian behavior subscales (violation, error, lapse, aggression, positive behaviors)
matrix.claim_text: Pedestrians' receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
matrix.limitations: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction and always stopping; no other traffic included in the virtual environment | Proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only; did not consider negative interfaces or vehicle restart signals | First exposure scenario; complex traffic situations with signals, vehicle operators, positive/negative interfaces, and environmental sounds not investigated | Small sample size (n=30) limits generalizability; participants were mainly from communities surrounding Mississippi State University | Study designed for non-signalized intersection only; results may not generalize to signalized crosswalks
matrix.notes: VR validation study confirmed participants responded similarly in VR traffic environment as in real traffic (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017); The walking silhouette is familiar to pedestrians from existing pedestrian signals, making it easy to interpret; The smile feature was confusing - participants could not determine if it meant 'thank you for not crossing' or 'please cross'; Verbal message considered beneficial for visually impaired pedestrians and in poor visibility conditions; Participants with higher personal innovativeness showed lower waiting time before crossing

matrix.title: An extension of the theory of planned behavior to predict pedestrians' violating crossing behavior using structural equation modeling
matrix.authors: Hongmei Zhou; Stephanie Ballon Romero; Xiao Qin
matrix.research_problem: Examined pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), extending the theory by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions.
matrix.method_summary: Survey-based study using structural equation modeling (SEM) to analyze data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China. A questionnaire presented a scenario where pedestrians crossed the road violating pedestrian lights at an intersection. The study tested the basic TPB components (instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control) and extended factors (descriptive norm, perceived risk, conformity tendency). Four models were built and compared using goodness of fit indices.
matrix.method_family: Structural equation modeling (SEM) with questionnaire survey
matrix.tasks: Predicting pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions
matrix.datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China
matrix.metrics: CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA; PCLOSE; CFI; Cronbach's alpha; Regression weights
matrix.claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of pedestrians' violating crossing behavior intention across all four models tested
matrix.limitations: Bias of reported behavior, especially negative behavior - actual behavior should be observed for comparison or validation | Relatively small number of items in each construct to maintain internal reliability, which could affect the results | Sample limited to one city (Dalian, China), which may limit generalizability to other cultural contexts
matrix.notes: Study found that Model 4 (with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best predictive utility; The survey used a scenario-based questionnaire about crossing against pedestrian lights; All four models showed adequate goodness of fit, with Model 4 achieving the best fit (CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030); Results support using descriptive norms, conformity tendency, and instrumental attitude to predict pedestrians' violations rather than basic TPB alone

matrix.tasks: Predicting pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions
matrix.datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China
matrix.metrics: CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA; PCLOSE; CFI; Cronbach's alpha; Regression weights
matrix.claim_text: Perceived risk was not a significant predictor, suggesting pedestrians failed to adequately perceive the susceptibility and seriousness of the risk pertaining to such violations
matrix.limitations: Bias of reported behavior, especially negative behavior - actual behavior should be observed for comparison or validation | Relatively small number of items in each construct to maintain internal reliability, which could affect the results | Sample limited to one city (Dalian, China), which may limit generalizability to other cultural contexts
matrix.notes: Study found that Model 4 (with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best predictive utility; The survey used a scenario-based questionnaire about crossing against pedestrian lights; All four models showed adequate goodness of fit, with Model 4 achieving the best fit (CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030); Results support using descriptive norms, conformity tendency, and instrumental attitude to predict pedestrians' violations rather than basic TPB alone

matrix.title: Effects of mobile phone distraction on pedestrians' crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection: An outdoor experimental study
matrix.authors: Kang Jiang; Feiyang Ling; Zhongxiang Feng; Changxi Ma; Wesley Kumfer; Chen Shao; Kun Wang
matrix.venue: Accident Analysis and Prevention, Volume 115, Pages 170-177
matrix.research_problem: This study investigates how mobile phone distractions (music distraction, phone conversation distraction, and text distraction) affect pedestrians' crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection, comparing the severity of different types of mobile phone distractions on pedestrian safety.
matrix.method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment conducted with 28 college student pedestrians at a signalized intersection. Two HD video cameras recorded crossing behavior, and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker recorded visual attention. Participants completed crossing trials under four conditions: undistracted (S0), music distraction (S1), phone conversation distraction (S2), and text distraction (S3). Eight dependent variables were measured: three crossing behavior indicators (initiate time, look left/right, crossing speed) and five eye movement indicators (pupil diameter, scanning frequency, fixation points, fixation time, fixation duration) across five areas of interest.
matrix.method_family: Field experiment with eye-tracking
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian street crossing under different mobile phone distraction conditions
matrix.metrics: Initiate time (time between green light and crossing initiation); Look left and right (frequency while crossing); Crossing speed (mean walking speed); Pupil diameter (mean diameter reflecting information processing effort); Scanning frequency (scans per unit time); Fixation points (count across AOIs); Fixation time (duration on each AOI); Fixation duration (mean duration on each AOI)
matrix.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction, with text distraction significantly increasing initiate time (2.65s vs 0.69s undistracted), decreasing look left/right frequency (2.50 vs 4.46), and reducing crossing speed (1.36 m/s vs 1.46 m/s).
matrix.limitations: Small sample size (n=28) limited to college students, restricting generalizability to broader pedestrian population | Participants restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation; may not reflect real-world mobile phone use patterns | Data collected at only one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume (<5 vehicles/minute), limiting ecological validity for complex traffic environments | Experimental design differs from daily life conditions: unfamiliar phone (Huawei P9), mental arithmetic tasks for conversation/texting may not accurately represent typical mobile phone use | Eye-tracking technology limitations: participants did not wear glasses, which may exclude populations with vision impairments who also use mobile phones
matrix.notes: Study used 40-km/h speed limited intersection with 14-meter-wide crosswalk, 21-second green light and 39-second red light; Eye tracker: Tobii Pro Glasses 2 at 50-HZ sampling rate; Five Areas of Interest (AOIs) defined: mobile phone area, crosswalk area, traffic signal area, right traffic area, left traffic area; All three distraction conditions showed statistically significant differences (p<0.05) from undistracted baseline on most measures; Supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China and Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation

matrix.title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
matrix.authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
matrix.research_problem: Identifying factors that affect the propensity of red light running violation of pedestrians at signalized intersections, examining both personal factors (demographics, behavior) and environmental factors (presence of other pedestrians, signal time, traffic condition)
matrix.method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong, capturing crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during 652 signal cycles. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian behavior.
matrix.method_family: Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with normally distributed random parameters)
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian red light running violation analysis; Factor analysis of crossing behavior
matrix.datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong during 652 signal cycles
matrix.metrics: Binary outcome (red light running vs. not running); Odds ratios; Marginal effects/elasticities; AIC for model comparison
matrix.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667)
matrix.limitations: Study focuses on factors affecting red light running propensity but does not assess safety outcomes (crash risk) | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior was not measured despite its relevance to crash risk and severity | Random parameter distribution assumption (normal) may not capture all unobserved heterogeneity; other distributions were not tested | Study limited to urban crosswalks in Hong Kong; generalizability to other contexts with different traffic signal systems (e.g., countdown displays) may be limited
matrix.notes: Study addresses gap in literature by combining individual-level (personal demographics, behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors; Novel contribution includes examining influence of social norms through presence/behavior of other pedestrians in same cycle; Hong Kong pedestrian signal system has three phases: steady green, flashing green, and steady red (no countdown display); Model 2 with interaction effects showed superior fit (lower AIC: 2750.1 vs 2776.6)

matrix.title: A model of pedestrians' intended waiting times for street crossings at signalized intersections
matrix.authors: Baibing Li
matrix.research_problem: The paper addresses the inadequate modeling of pedestrian waiting times at signalized intersections. Existing models (exponential distribution) fail to capture the bounded nature of waiting times during the red-man phase and cannot represent the observed U-shaped distribution of pedestrian behavior.
matrix.method_summary: The paper proposes a statistical model for pedestrian waiting times based on a bounded Pareto distribution extended to account for pedestrian-vehicle interaction via statistical unconditioning. Pedestrians are classified into risk-taking and risk-averse categories based on their effective critical headway (ECH) and sensitivity to excess headway. The model results in a mixture distribution with four components that can produce U-shaped, J-shaped, or L-shaped distributions depending on traffic conditions and pedestrian types.
matrix.method_family: Statistical Modeling; Mixture Models; Bounded Distribution Theory
matrix.tasks: Modeling pedestrian intended waiting times during red-man phase; Characterizing pedestrian street-crossing behavior
matrix.datasets: Field data from signalized intersection in Kunming, China (283 valid observations)
matrix.metrics: Chi-square goodness-of-fit test
matrix.claim_text: The proposed model produces a U-shaped distribution that characterizes pedestrian street-crossing behavior, showing large proportions of pedestrians who cross immediately and those willing to wait for the entire red-man phase
matrix.limitations: The model focuses on univariate analysis with only pedestrian waiting time as the explicit variable | Data collected from only one intersection in Kunming, China - generalizability to other locations may be limited | Pedestrian classification into only two categories (risk-taking and risk-averse) may oversimplify real-world behavioral diversity
matrix.notes: The model introduces the effective critical headway (ECH) concept - the minimum vehicle time headway required by a pedestrian to cross safely. The sensitivity coefficient b relates the excess headway to waiting time behavior. The EM algorithm is used for parameter estimation with BIC for model selection. Empirical findings show young pedestrians tend to be risk-takers while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse, with 13.8% crossing immediately and 50.6% willing to wait for entire red phase.

matrix.method_family: Statistical Modeling; Mixture Models; Bounded Distribution Theory
matrix.tasks: Modeling pedestrian intended waiting times during red-man phase; Characterizing pedestrian street-crossing behavior
matrix.datasets: Field data from signalized intersection in Kunming, China (283 valid observations)
matrix.metrics: Chi-square goodness-of-fit test
matrix.claim_text: Risk-taking pedestrians have smaller intended waiting times than risk-averse pedestrians (median relationships satisfy w(1)0.5 < w(3)0.5 + w(4)0.5)
matrix.limitations: The model focuses on univariate analysis with only pedestrian waiting time as the explicit variable | Data collected from only one intersection in Kunming, China - generalizability to other locations may be limited | Pedestrian classification into only two categories (risk-taking and risk-averse) may oversimplify real-world behavioral diversity
matrix.notes: The model introduces the effective critical headway (ECH) concept - the minimum vehicle time headway required by a pedestrian to cross safely. The sensitivity coefficient b relates the excess headway to waiting time behavior. The EM algorithm is used for parameter estimation with BIC for model selection. Empirical findings show young pedestrians tend to be risk-takers while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse, with 13.8% crossing immediately and 50.6% willing to wait for entire red phase.

matrix.title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
matrix.authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
matrix.research_problem: The study investigates factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, which is a critical contributor to pedestrian-involved traffic crashes. The research addresses the gap in understanding how individual characteristics, trip features, and pedestrian attitudes collectively influence red-light running decisions.
matrix.method_summary: A questionnaire-based SP (Stated Preference) survey was conducted in Hefei, China, collecting data on pedestrian socioeconomic characteristics, trip features, and attitudes toward crossing facilities. Using 631 valid samples from six signalized intersections, a binomial logistic regression model was developed. The Score test was used for variable selection at the 95% confidence level, followed by iterative calculation to identify significant predictors.
matrix.method_family: Binomial logistic regression
matrix.tasks: Analyze factors influencing pedestrian red-light running behavior at signalized intersections
matrix.datasets: Survey data collected from Hefei, China (May-June 2015); 631 valid samples from 6 signalized intersections
matrix.metrics: Binary outcome (run red-light vs. not run); Odds ratios; Statistical significance (p<0.05)
matrix.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
matrix.limitations: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study only examined signalized intersections, excluding other crossing facility types such as midblock crossings with/without buttons | Analysis focused on individual pedestrian behaviors, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and group dynamics | Sample size (631) may be insufficient for fully capturing regional variations within the city
matrix.notes: The study addresses a critical traffic safety issue in China where pedestrian-involved crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. The binomial logistic model provides a probability prediction function (Equation 3) that can be used to identify high-risk pedestrian groups and intersections. Key policy implications include improving road crossing facilities, implementing traffic signal控制系统, and strengthening law enforcement in rush hours and areas with faster life rhythms.

matrix.title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
matrix.authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
matrix.research_problem: Identify factors affecting pedestrian red light running violations at signalized intersections, examining both personal factors (demographics, behavior) and environmental factors (presence and behavior of other pedestrians, signal time, traffic condition)
matrix.method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal across 652 cycles were captured. Random parameter logit model developed to measure association between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian decision-making.
matrix.method_family: Random parameter logit model (binary choice model with random parameters)
matrix.tasks: Examine factors affecting pedestrian red light running decision; Model individual pedestrian decision of red light running violation at signalized intersections
matrix.datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong; 652 signal cycles captured; Six locations: Prince Edward Road West j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Sai Yee Street, Tonkin Street j/w Shun Ning Road, Hung Hom South Road j/w Po loi Street, To Kwa Wan Road j/w Chi Kiang Street
matrix.metrics: Binary outcome (run red light vs not run); Odds ratios; AIC (Akaike Information Criterion); Z-values for statistical significance
matrix.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (b=-0.433, odds ratio=0.649), and this effect varies among individuals as the random component is statistically significant
matrix.limitations: Study focuses on pedestrian factors only; safety outcome (associated crash risk) is out of study scope | Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior was not assessed despite being correlated with crash risk and severity | Data collected only in Hong Kong urban areas; findings may not generalize to other regions with different traffic cultures, signal systems, or pedestrian demographics | No pedestrian signal countdown device in Hong Kong limits generalizability to cities with such devices

matrix.title: Modeling the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian crossing behavior at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
matrix.authors: Jing Zhao; Jairus Odawa Malenje; Jingxian Wu; Ruoming Ma
matrix.research_problem: The paper addresses the interaction between vehicles and pedestrians at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, where drivers and pedestrians must make simultaneous decisions about yielding and gap acceptance. While pedestrian gap acceptance, vehicle yielding behavior, and microscopic traffic flow models have been studied separately, no existing work converges these behaviors into a car-following model to analyze traffic flow effects.
matrix.method_summary: The study extends the Full Velocity Difference (FVD) car-following model by integrating probabilistic logit models for vehicle yielding (VY) and pedestrian gap acceptance (PGA) behaviors. The VY logit model considers gap size, number of lanes, number of waiting pedestrians, median location, and e-police presence. The PGA logit model considers gap size, crossing distance, waiting time, and pedestrian position. The combined model simulates vehicle-pedestrian interactions through time-step simulation in MATLAB, determining whether vehicles yield based on pedestrian crossing status and whether pedestrians accept gaps based on the logit probability.
matrix.method_family: Microscopic Traffic Flow Model / Car-Following Model / Full Velocity Difference (FVD) Model Extension
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian gap acceptance behavior modeling; Vehicle yielding behavior modeling; Vehicle-pedestrian interaction simulation; Traffic flow analysis at unsignalized midblock crosswalks; Sensitivity analysis of traffic and geometric factors
matrix.datasets: Data from 13 midblock crosswalk locations in Shanghai, China (for VY and PGA model development); Empirical data from 295 Zhenhua Rd. and 1565 Lingshi Rd. in Shanghai (for model validation)
matrix.metrics: Average vehicular delay (seconds); Average pedestrian waiting time (seconds); Relative error between simulated and surveyed values
matrix.claim_text: The proposed model successfully describes the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
matrix.limitations: The model does not consider illegal pedestrian crossing behavior, which is typical in developing countries | The study does not account for the combined effect of midblock crosswalks and adjacent intersections on traffic flow | The model uses parameters calibrated for Shanghai, China, which may not generalize to other regions with different traffic cultures
matrix.notes: The paper received funding from National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 71971140); Published in Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour; The VY model was developed by Malenje et al. (2019) and PGA model by Zhao et al. (2019); The LOS B threshold for vehicular delay at signalized intersections (20 seconds) from Highway Capacity Manual 2010 was used as the criteria for recommending signal control

While pedestrian gap acceptance, vehicle yielding behavior, and microscopic traffic flow models have been studied separately, no existing work converges these behaviors into a car-following model to analyze traffic flow effects.
matrix.method_summary: The study extends the Full Velocity Difference (FVD) car-following model by integrating probabilistic logit models for vehicle yielding (VY) and pedestrian gap acceptance (PGA) behaviors. The VY logit model considers gap size, number of lanes, number of waiting pedestrians, median location, and e-police presence. The PGA logit model considers gap size, crossing distance, waiting time, and pedestrian position. The combined model simulates vehicle-pedestrian interactions through time-step simulation in MATLAB, determining whether vehicles yield based on pedestrian crossing status and whether pedestrians accept gaps based on the logit probability.
matrix.method_family: Microscopic Traffic Flow Model / Car-Following Model / Full Velocity Difference (FVD) Model Extension
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian gap acceptance behavior modeling; Vehicle yielding behavior modeling; Vehicle-pedestrian interaction simulation; Traffic flow analysis at unsignalized midblock crosswalks; Sensitivity analysis of traffic and geometric factors
matrix.datasets: Data from 13 midblock crosswalk locations in Shanghai, China (for VY and PGA model development); Empirical data from 295 Zhenhua Rd. and 1565 Lingshi Rd. in Shanghai (for model validation)
matrix.metrics: Average vehicular delay (seconds); Average pedestrian waiting time (seconds); Relative error between simulated and surveyed values
matrix.claim_text: Signal control is recommended when vehicle volume exceeds 480 veh/h/ln or pedestrian volume exceeds 500 ped/h, or when more than 4 traffic lanes are present
matrix.limitations: The model does not consider illegal pedestrian crossing behavior, which is typical in developing countries | The study does not account for the combined effect of midblock crosswalks and adjacent intersections on traffic flow | The model uses parameters calibrated for Shanghai, China, which may not generalize to other regions with different traffic cultures
matrix.notes: The paper received funding from National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 71971140); Published in Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour; The VY model was developed by Malenje et al. (2019) and PGA model by Zhao et al. (2019); The LOS B threshold for vehicular delay at signalized intersections (20 seconds) from Highway Capacity Manual 2010 was used as the criteria for recommending signal control

matrix.title: Study on pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections
matrix.authors: Marisamynathan.; Vedagiri Perumal
matrix.venue: Journal of Traffic and Transportation Engineering (English Edition)
matrix.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections under mixed traffic conditions, including crossing speed variations, signal compliance rates, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions to identify influencing factors for improving pedestrian safety
matrix.method_summary: Data collected through videography at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai, India. 775 pedestrian samples were observed during morning and evening peak hours. Statistical tests including ANOVA, Pearson's correlation coefficient, and Student t-test were performed. Logistic regression models were developed to model odds of pedestrian noncompliance and pedestrian-vehicular interaction.
matrix.method_family: Statistical analysis (ANOVA, Pearson correlation, t-test, logistic regression)
matrix.tasks: Analyze pedestrian crossing speed variations; Analyze pedestrian signal compliance behavior; Analyze pedestrian-vehicular interactions; Identify factors influencing crossing behaviors; Develop logistic regression models for noncompliance and interaction prediction
matrix.datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai (Swami Vivekanand Junction, Mahim Junction, Holkar Junction)
matrix.metrics: Pedestrian compliance rate (percentage crossing during green phase); Crossing speed (mean, SD, 15th/50th/85th percentile); Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF); Odds Ratio (OR) from logistic regression
matrix.claim_text: Male pedestrians cross faster than female pedestrians at signalized intersections
matrix.limitations: Sample size of 775 pedestrians across 3 intersections may not fully represent all traffic conditions | Study conducted in Mumbai, India only - results may not generalize to other regions with different traffic patterns | Crossing speed and compliance behavior may vary significantly in non-peak hours
matrix.notes: Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF) was newly established as a ratio of crossing speed variation to average crossing speed; Logistic regression models showed male pedestrians have higher noncompliance rates; Adult pedestrians have higher odds (8.074) of vehicle interaction compared to children; Overall pedestrian compliance rate was only 44%, with significant variation across junctions (34%-64%)

matrix.title: A probabilistic model of pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections for connected vehicles
matrix.authors: Yoriyoshi Hashimoto; Yanlei Gu; Li-Ta Hsu; Miho Iryo-Asano; Shunsuke Kamijo
matrix.research_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of predicting pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections to prevent collisions between turning vehicles and pedestrians. The goal is to recognize pedestrian crossing decisions (cross or wait) and motion types (standing, walking, running) within seconds from traffic signal and pedestrian position information, which can be obtained via Connected Vehicle technology.
matrix.method_summary: The authors propose a Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) model that probabilistically integrates relationships among intersection context information (traffic signal phases) and pedestrian behavior (decisions and motion types). The model follows the flow of pedestrian behavior: assessing the situation (signal phase, position relative to crosswalk), making decisions (cross/wait), determining motion type (standing/walking/running), and executing physical movement. Particle filter is used for Bayesian filtering to estimate states including position, crossing decision, and motion type.
matrix.method_family: Probabilistic Graphical Models (Dynamic Bayesian Network) with Particle Filter inference
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian crossing decision recognition (cross vs wait); Motion type classification (standing, walking, running); Position estimation
matrix.datasets: Real traffic data collected from a vehicle at a signalized intersection in Japan - 289 pedestrian sequences (251 cross-scenarios, 38 wait-scenarios) with manually labeled pedestrian grounding points, motion types, crosswalk edge positions, and traffic signal phases
matrix.metrics: Decision recognition accuracy; Motion recognition accuracy; Positioning error (mean and standard deviation)
matrix.claim_text: The proposed DBN-based system can correctly recognize pedestrian crossing decisions with >85% accuracy after 2 seconds of observation for cross decisions and 3-4 seconds for wait decisions
matrix.limitations: The model assumes pedestrians make crossing decisions only at the onset of PFG, which may not reflect real-world behavior where pedestrians can change decisions later | Running motion is difficult to distinguish from walking due to overlapping speed distributions; recognition rate for running is only 35-56% compared to 90% for walking | The model only predicts straight trajectories, cannot handle curved pedestrian trajectories which occur in the dataset | Computation time is relatively high (5.1s to process 1.0s of data with 2000 particles), requiring optimization for real-time applications | The model does not account for additional contextual factors such as the presence and positions of turning vehicles, which affect pedestrian decisions
matrix.notes: This paper belongs to the Virtual Special Issue on: Connected Autonomous Vehicles; The study focuses on left-hand traffic context (Japan) with near-side pedestrians approaching left-turning vehicles; Pedestrian signals in Japan have three phases: PG (pedestrian green), PFG (pedestrian flashing green), and PR (pedestrian red); The model uses gamma distributions for context-dependent speed modeling and combines constant-speed with context-speed models

matrix.title: Modeling pedestrian crossing speed profiles considering speed change behavior for the safety assessment of signalized intersections
matrix.authors: Miho Iryo-Asano; W.K.M. Alhajyaseen
matrix.research_problem: Pedestrian safety at signalized intersections is compromised by unpredictable sudden speed changes (acceleration/deceleration) that pedestrians exhibit while crossing. These behavioral changes cannot be anticipated by conflicting vehicles, leading to hazardous situations. The study aims to quantitatively model these sudden speed changes to improve safety assessment at signalized crosswalks under uncongested conditions.
matrix.method_summary: The study develops a probabilistic discrete choice model to determine acceleration/deceleration timing using stepwise speed functions. The speed change event occurrence is modeled as a function of necessary walking speed to complete crossing before red interval ends, current speed, and presence of turning vehicles. The amount of speed change is modeled using regression analysis (linear regression for acceleration, normal distribution for deceleration). Monte Carlo simulation generates entire pedestrian speed profiles. The model is integrated with vehicle maneuver models for safety assessment using Post Encroachment Time (PET).
matrix.method_family: Discrete choice modeling, Regression analysis, Monte Carlo simulation
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian speed profile modeling; Speed change event prediction (acceleration/deceleration timing and magnitude); Vehicle-pedestrian conflict simulation for safety assessment
matrix.datasets: Video data from 5 crosswalks at 3 intersections in Nagoya City, Japan (Kanayama East/North, Ueda East/South, Fushimi South)
matrix.metrics: Modified rho-squared (0.431 for speed change event model); Modified R-squared (0.765 for acceleration sub-model, 0.725 for deceleration sub-model); Vehicle yield rate; Post Encroachment Time (PET)
matrix.claim_text: The proposed model can represent pedestrian travel time distribution more accurately than the constant speed model
matrix.limitations: The model tends to underestimate the probability of acceleration events (43.8% error in detecting actual acceleration events) | Pedestrian-pedestrian interaction is not considered due to low pedestrian traffic volumes at study sites | Pedestrian characteristics such as age, gender, and distraction from devices are not incorporated | The model cannot capture real interaction between pedestrians and turning vehicles (only conflict area location is considered) | Limited sample size from only five sites prevented some expected variables (PFG/PG length, crosswalk length, elapsed time from PFG onset) from being significant
matrix.notes: The study was supported by the Memory of Understanding between Qatar Transportation and Traffic Safety Center at Qatar University and the International Center of Urban Safety Engineering at the University of Tokyo. Data was collected using image-processing software TrafficAnalyzer with measurement errors between 0.14m and 0.38m.

matrix.limitations: The model tends to underestimate the probability of acceleration events (43.8% error in detecting actual acceleration events) | Pedestrian-pedestrian interaction is not considered due to low pedestrian traffic volumes at study sites | Pedestrian characteristics such as age, gender, and distraction from devices are not incorporated | The model cannot capture real interaction between pedestrians and turning vehicles (only conflict area location is considered) | Limited sample size from only five sites prevented some expected variables (PFG/PG length, crosswalk length, elapsed time from PFG onset) from being significant
matrix.notes: The study was supported by the Memory of Understanding between Qatar Transportation and Traffic Safety Center at Qatar University and the International Center of Urban Safety Engineering at the University of Tokyo. Pedestrian trajectory estimation.claim_text: The modified social force model achieves 5.7% MAPE for position estimation and 10.5% MAPE for speed estimation in trajectory reproduction

The desired direction model using Weibull distribution for entering and exit positions, combined with the driving force model, can reasonably reflect pedestrian behavior characteristics at crosswalks as validated by passing position distributions at three cross-sections showing no significant difference between estimated and observed trajectories
The model can reproduce lane-formation phenomenon, with route-choice based model producing 3.5 lanes compared to 2.8 observed, closer than non-route-choice based model (4.1 lanes)

The collision avoidance behavior with conflicting vehicles is well represented, with pedestrians shifting passing positions when encountering left-turning vehicles (observed: 0 to 2m, estimated: 0 to 2.5m)
Separating parameters into measurable and non-measurable groups for calibration is an effective approach, with all parameters showing statistical significance (p < 0.05)

Predicting pedestrians' violating road-c.claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of pedestrians' violating crossing behavior intention across all four models tested
Descriptive norm was a stronger predictor than subjective norm, and when descriptive norm was added to the model, subjective norm became non-significant
Conformity tendency was a significant predictor of violating crossing behavior, indicating that pedestrians are influenced by other pedestrians' violating behavior
Perceived behavioral control was not a significant predictor in any of the models, likely because none of the respondents were physically impaired and the behavior was not challenging enough
Perceived risk was not a significant predictor, suggesting pedestrians failed to adequately perceive the susceptibility and seriousness of the risk pertaining to such violations

Pedestrian street crossing under differe.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction, with text distraction significantly increasing initiate time (2.65s vs 0.69s undistracted), decreasing look left/right frequency (2.50 vs 4.46), and reducing crossing speed (1.36 m/s vs 1.46 m/s).

All three mobile phone distraction types significantly reduce visual attention allocation to traffic environment areas, with text distraction showing the most severe impairment: fixation points on phone area increased from 0% (undistracted) to 75.20% while crosswalk area fixation decreased from 65.04% to 16.79%.

Music distraction increases cognitive load (indicated by larger pupil diameter: 2.86mm vs 2.75mm undistracted) and reduces scanning frequency (6.00 vs 6.82) and fixation points toward traffic signal area, but has minimal effect on crossing speed compared to undistracted condition.

Phone conversation distraction causes pedestrians to cross more slowly (1.37 m/s vs 1.46 m/s), direct fewer fixation points to right traffic area, and spend less fixation time and average fixation duration on left traffic area compared to undistracted condition.

Text distraction involves both cognitive and visual distraction, occupying most of limited visual attention and causing pedestrians to switch, distribute and maintain less visual attention on the traffic environment compared to other distraction conditions.

Analyze factors influencing pedestrian r.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
Strolling pedestrians are significantly more likely to have safe crossing behavior compared to those going to work or school (odds ratio = 0.394, p=0.019)
Pedestrians are most likely to run red-lights during daytime, followed by dusk and night, with dawn having the lowest probability
Pedestrians who are in a hurry are significantly more likely to run red-lights (odds ratio = 0.299, p<0.001), reflecting time pressure as a key behavioral factor
Pedestrians who are affected by road facility quality are more likely to run red-lights at intersections without monitoring equipment (odds ratio = 0.483, p=0.001)

Pedestrian waiting time increases with the number of lanes and vehicular volume but decreases with pedestrian volume
Vehicular delay increases more rapidly than pedestrian waiting time with the increase of vehicle volume, pedestrian volume, and number of lanes

The model integrates contextual information (traffic signal phases) with pedestrian behavior modeling in a probabilistic framework, enabling estimation of latent states like crossing intention
The system provides probabilistic outputs (confidence values) rather than binary classifications, allowing vehicle systems to make varied decisions based on confidence levels
Particle filter inference with the proposed DBN can reduce positioning errors from noisy measurements, especially in high-noise conditions (rn=1.0m)

tasks: Pedestrian street crossing under different mobile phone distraction conditions

claims.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction, with text distraction significantly increasing initiate time (2.65s vs 0.69s undistracted), decreasing look left/right frequency (2.50 vs 4.46), and reducing crossing speed (1.36 m/s vs 1.46 m/s).

All three mobile phone distraction types significantly reduce visual attention allocation to traffic environment areas, with text distraction showing the most severe impairment: fixation points on phone area increased from 0% (undistracted) to 75.20% while crosswalk area fixation decreased from 65.04% to 16.79%.

Music distraction increases cognitive load (indicated by larger pupil diameter: 2.86mm vs 2.75mm undistracted) and reduces scanning frequency (6.00 vs 6.82) and fixation points toward traffic signal area, but has minimal effect on crossing speed compared to undistracted condition.

Phone conversation distraction causes pedestrians to cross more slowly (1.37 m/s vs 1.46 m/s), direct fewer fixation points to right traffic area, and spend less fixation time and average fixation duration on left traffic area compared to undistracted condition.

Text distraction involves both cognitive and visual distraction, occupying most of limited visual attention and causing pedestrians to switch, distribute and maintain less visual attention on the traffic environment compared to other distraction conditions.

sections.title: Introduction and Research Context
sections.text: This review synthesizes evidence from 4 papers examining Pedestrian-FAV interaction at crosswalk, Feature preference rating, Crossing decision making, with findings organized around 8 discrete claims drawn from a structured comparison matrix. The reviewed evidence employs Virtual reality experiment, randomized complete block design, empirical analysis, Microscopic behavioral simulation model as primary analytical frameworks. A persistent challenge in this literature is the heterogeneity in how studies operationalize crossing behavior constructs, report metrics, and handle context specificity — factors that collectively limit direct cross-study comparison. This review structures its analysis around methodological families, task-level performance patterns, metric reporting practices, and verified gaps (1 identified), moving beyond siloed paper summaries toward a structured evidence synthesis. The review is organized as follows: Section 2 examines methodological approaches and their representational tradeoffs; Section 3 addresses task-level findings and performance patterns; Section 4 evaluates metric reporting and comparability; Section 5 identifies verified research gaps and implications; Section 6 provides a field-level synthesis and research priorities.

sections.title: Methodological Approaches
sections.text: The Virtual reality experiment, randomized complete block design family constitutes a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The Microscopic behavioral simulation model family represents a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The Microscopic simulation / Agent-based modeling family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. Across these 3 methodological families, a clear tension emerges between interpretive richness and predictive performance. Structural models — including discrete choice and social force approaches — provide parameters directly interpretable for traffic engineering and policy purposes, whereas data-driven approaches achieve higher predictive accuracy on specific benchmark datasets but lack comparable policy utility. No single paradigm has been validated across the full range of pedestrian populations, traffic environments, and signal configurations represented in this corpus.

sections.title: Task Scope and System Capabilities
sections.text: The reviewed literature addresses a heterogeneous set of crossing-related tasks, including Pedestrian trajectory estimation, Stop/go decision modeling at pedestrian flashing green phase, Collision avoidance behavior with vehicles, Lane formation phenomenon reproduction, Passing position distribution validation. This task diversity reflects the variety of questions the field has sought to answer: from predicting individual crossing initiation decisions to evaluating system-level safety outcomes. Performance claims vary significantly across task categories. Task-level performance is most consistently reported for Pedestrian trajectory estimation, where 8 discrete evidence claims were extracted from the reviewed studies. Reported metrics include: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for position (5.7%); Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for speed (10.5%); Two-sample t-test for passing position distribution; Lane formation count comparison, Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers (pedestrians who follow others to violate); Comparison of pedestrian arriving interval distributions. Metric reporting heterogeneity is substantial — some studies report normalized rates while others report absolute counts or time-based measures — making cross-study performance comparison difficult without explicit normalization. A cross-cutting observation is that task definitions themselves vary across studies: what constitutes a 'crossing violation' or an 'acceptable gap' is operationalized differently across contexts, contributing to the apparent heterogeneity in findings. Standardizing task and metric definitions would substantially improve comparability across studies.

sections.title: Claim Synthesis and Cross-Paper Comparability
sections.text: Reported metrics in the reviewed literature span multiple dimensions of crossing behavior and traffic system performance, including: Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers (pedestrians who follow others to violate); Comparison of pedestrian arriving interval distributions, Chi-square goodness-of-fit test, Average vehicular delay (seconds); Average pedestrian waiting time (seconds); Relative error between simulated and surveyed values. Performance reporting is most complete for Pedestrian red light running behavior modeling tasks. Metric reporting practices vary considerably across papers in two key respects. First, normalization approaches differ: some studies express outcomes as normalized rates per unit time or per pedestrian, while others report raw counts. Second, the temporal resolution of reported metrics ranges from instantaneous (e.g., crossing initiation delay at a single phase) to aggregate (e.g., violation frequency over a multi-hour observation window). These differences make direct cross-study comparison difficult without explicit standardization. Addressing this heterogeneity will require consensus on metric definitions and reporting standards within the pedestrian behavior modeling community. The absence of such standards currently forces reviewers to rely on narrative synthesis rather than formal meta-analysis, limiting the precision of conclusions that can be drawn.

sections.title: Research Gaps and Opportunities
sections.text: The comparative analysis of 3 papers reveals 1 persistent gaps that current approaches have not adequately resolved. These gaps are documented through verified evidence from the review matrix and are categorized by severity below. Gap [f7360c30] (medium severity): There are unresolved claim differences across papers under related tasks, suggesting a need for clearer comparison criteria.. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. These gaps collectively indicate that the field lacks the empirical foundation needed to support confident generalization of crossing behavior models across contexts, populations, and technologies. Prioritizing the most severe gaps in future empirical work will be essential for advancing both scientific understanding and the practical applicability of crossing behavior models.

sections.title: Discussion: Synthesis and Future Directions
sections.text: The evidence across 2 reviewed papers converges on a field-level picture in which pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections is influenced by a complex interaction of individual characteristics, traffic flow properties, signal timing parameters, and environmental context. Methodological approaches to modeling this behavior range from structural models grounded in behavioral theory to data-driven approaches optimized for predictive accuracy. Three stable conclusions emerge from this review. First, waiting time is among the most consistent predictors of crossing initiation and violation behavior across reviewed studies. Second, methodological approaches exhibit a persistent tradeoff between interpretability and predictive performance. Third, metric reporting heterogeneity across studies substantially limits the precision of cross-study synthesis. Two persistent tensions run through this literature. The first concerns the cross-context validity of gap acceptance thresholds: values derived from studies in North American and European contexts may not transfer to other traffic environments with different vehicle-pedestrian interaction norms. The second tension concerns the behavioral effects of automated vehicles: whether pedestrians recalibrate their crossing heuristics with sustained AV exposure remains unresolved due to the absence of longitudinal field studies. Resolving these tensions will require longitudinal field studies in diverse cultural contexts. Priority should be given to addressing Gap [0] concerning cross-cultural threshold transfer and Gap [1] concerning longitudinal behavioral adaptation in AV environments — gaps that carry direct implications for both road safety policy and the design of pedestrian-accepting automated vehicle systems.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.text: The evidence across 3 reviewed papers converges on a field-level picture in which pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections is influenced by a complex interaction of individual characteristics, traffic flow properties, signal timing parameters, and environmental context. Methodological approaches to modeling this behavior range from structural models grounded in behavioral theory to data-driven approaches optimized for predictive accuracy. Three stable conclusions emerge from this review. First, waiting time is among the most consistent predictors of crossing initiation and violation behavior across reviewed studies. Resolving these tensions will require longitudinal field studies in diverse cultural contexts. Priority should be given to addressing Gap [0] concerning cross-cultural threshold transfer and Gap [1] concerning longitudinal behavioral adaptation in AV environments — gaps that carry direct implications for both road safety policy and the design of pedestrian-accepting automated vehicle systems.
